coredata.dc:title: Facile construction of fluorescent traceable prodrug nanosponges for tumor intracellular pH/hypoxia dual-triggered drug delivery
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Wei
Xie, Pengwei
Pei, Mingliang
Li, Guoping
Wang, Zhiyuan
Liu, Peng
coredata.dc:description: Drug delivery systems (DDSs) have become hotpot in tumor chemotherapy to improve anti-tumor efficacy. It is still a challenge to control the diameter and distribution of the drug carriers. High performance reactions are expected to favor the precise design of the polymer structure, as well as the final crosslinked nanoparticles. Here, a facile approach was established to prepare the fluorescent traceable prodrug nanosponges for tumor intracellular pH/hypoxia dual-triggered drug delivery, via a high-performance reaction of isocyanate groups with fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQD) as building block. The proposed doxorubicin (DOX) conjugated CQD-based nanosponges (DOX-Hy-CQD(ssPEGss)n) possessed excellent acid/GSH dual-triggered drug release, due to the dual-triggered disintegration inside tumor cells. As a result, the conjugated drug was released with an enhanced selective inhibition tumor cell proliferation than the free DOX. Furthermore, the fluorescence of the disintegrated CQD-contained small species might enable the real-time in-site imaging during drug release.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Drug delivery system
Polyprodrug nanosponges
pH/hypoxia dual-triggered
Fluorescent traceable

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication and characterization of CQD(ssPEGss)n nanosponges
Fabrication and characterization of DOX-Hy-CQD(ssPEGss)n prodrug nanosponges
In vitro cytotoxicity and intracellular distribution

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of CQD (a), H-CQD (b), CQD(ssPEGss)n-1:3 (c), Hy-CQD(ssPEGss)n (d) and DOX-Hy-CQD(ssPEGss)n (e).

Hydrodynamic diameters and distributions of CQD (a), H-CQD (b), CQD(ssPEGss)n-1:3 (c), Hy-CQD(ssPEGss)n (d) and DOX-Hy-CQD(ssPEGss)n (e) in aqueous dispersions and DOX-Hy-CQD(ssPEGss)n in pH 7.4 PBS +10 μM GSH for 12 h (e).

Cumulative drug release from the DOX-Hy-CQD(ssPEGss)n in different releasing media (a) and cytotoxicity of Hy-CQD(ssPEGss)n, DOX-Hy-CQD(ssPEGss)n at DOX-equivalent dose and DOX on the HepG2 and L02 cells (b). The statistical significance was set at *p < 0.05.
UV–vis spectra of CQD, H-CQD, CQD(ssPEGss)n-1:3, Hy-CQD(ssPEGss)n, DOX-Hy-CQD(ssPEGss)n and DOX.

Fluorescence emission spectra of CQD (a), H-CQD (b), CQD(ssPEGss)n-1:3 (c), Hy-CQD(ssPEGss)n (d) and DOX-Hy-CQD(ssPEGss)n (e) in aqueous dispersions (0.05 mg/mL), DOX-Hy-CQD(ssPEGss)n (0.05 mg/mL) in pH 5.0 ABS +10 mM GSH for different times at λex = 360 nm (f).

CLSM images of HepG2 cells after incubation with DOX-Hy-CQD(ssPEGss)n. The label a, b and c represent the images of the channels of DAPI, DOX and CQD, d is the merged images of a and b, e is the merged images of a and c, f is the merged images of b and c.
Fabrication of the fluorescent traceable prodrug nanosponges.

head.ce:title: Facile construction of fluorescent traceable prodrug nanosponges for tumor intracellular pH/hypoxia dual-triggered drug delivery

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qinghai Normal University, Xining 810016, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qinghai Normal University, Xining 810016, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Drug delivery systems (DDSs) have become hotpot in tumor chemotherapy to improve anti-tumor efficacy. It is still a challenge to control the diameter and distribution of the drug carriers. High performance reactions are expected to favor the precise design of the polymer structure, as well as the final crosslinked nanoparticles. Here, a facile approach was established to prepare the fluorescent traceable prodrug nanosponges for tumor intracellular pH/hypoxia dual-triggered drug delivery, via a high-performance reaction of isocyanate groups with fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQD) as building block. The proposed doxorubicin (DOX) conjugated CQD-based nanosponges (DOX-Hy-CQD(ssPEGss)n) possessed excellent acid/GSH dual-triggered drug release, due to the dual-triggered disintegration inside tumor cells. As a result, the conjugated drug was released with an enhanced selective inhibition tumor cell proliferation than the free DOX. Furthermore, the fluorescence of the disintegrated CQD-contained small species might enable the real-time in-site imaging during drug release.

ce:section.ce:para: Polymer prodrugs have attracted more and more insterests in tumor chemotherapy [1]. To repond to the specific tumor intercellular microenvironment, the chemotherapeutics have been usually conjugated onto the side or end groups via dynamic covalent congjugations, such as acid-labile [2], glutathione (GSH) or reactive oxygen species (ROS) triggered cleavable ones [3]. The nanoscaled DDSs could be fabricated by self-assembling the amphiphilic copolymer prodrugs [4], crosslinking the polymer prodrugs or conjugating the drugs onto polymer nanoparticles [5]. By now, it is still a challenge to control the diameter of the final nanomedicines to favor the passive targeting via the EPR effect.

It is well-known that the high performance reactions favor the precise design of the polymer structure. Therefore, the ideal DDSs with the desired morphology and diameter could be easily achieved. For example, polyurethanes with stoichiometric structures could be synthesized as potential drug delivery vehicle for cancer therapy, via the high performance reaction of isocyanate groups with hydroxyl groups [6].

Besides the aim to realize the tumor on-demand drug release by efficient stimuli-responsiveness, functional inorganic nanomaterials have also been incorporated into the DDSs for the tumor-targeting or diagnosis function [7]. Recently, CQDs have been intensely investigated in bioimaging owing to their low cost, easy access to materials and preparation, as well as its good hydrophilicity, stability, biocompatibility, low toxicity, excellent photoluminescence and light stability [8]. Their plentiful surface functional groups endow the facile functionalization including drug loading or conjugating [9]. However, they could not be used directly due to their very small size for rapid renal clearance [10]. Therefore, various CQD-based nanocomposites were designed as DDSs for tumor theranostic applications [11].

Here, a facile approach was established to prepare the fluorescent traceable prodrug nanosponges for the tumor-specific pH/hypoxia dual-triggered drug delivery, via a high performance reaction of isocyanate groups (Scheme 1 ). Besides the role as fluorescent building blocks in the bioreducible degraded nanosponges, the multifunctional CQDs also acted as the acid-labile conjuagting sites for DOX. With the unique structure, the proposed prodrug nanosponges are expected to be traced in the human body and disintegrate only in the acidic and hypoxia tumor intracellular microenvironment for the selective theranostic application.

The N-doped carbon quantum dots (CQD) were synthesized by the microwave-assisted pyrolysis of citric acid and ethylenediamine [12]. Then the H-CQD were designed by the reaction of the surface amino groups on the CQD with p-hydroxybenzaldehyde (Scheme S1): 50 mg CQD was dispersed in 20 mL dimethyl sulfone (DMSO) solution. 80 mg p-hydroxybenzaldehyde and 2 drops of acetic acid were added, and then the mixture was stirred in dark at 50 °C for 24 h. Finally, the H-CQD was collected as light-yellow powder by dialysis (MW = 1000) with water for 5 days, and then lyophilization.

The CQD(ssPEGss)n nanosponges was then fabricated via the crosslinking of the isocyanate-terminated polyurethane prepolymer (Scheme S2) with the H-CQD. Firstly, the ssPEGss was synthesized via a one-pot two-step method: 10 mL of polyethylene glycol (PEG-600, 1.0 mmol) solution in N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) was dropped into 20 mL of hexamethylene diisocyanate (HDI) (2.0 mmol) solution in DMF with stirring and then the mixture was stirred at 85 °C for 3 h. After cooling to 50 °C, 2.4 mmol of bis(2-hydroxyethyl) disulfide was added and stirred at 85 °C for 3 h and overnight at room temperature. The ssPEGss was collected by precipitation in a mixture of tetrahydrofuran and ether (1:4) for 3 times and drying under vacuum. Then the CQD(ssPEGss)n nanosponges were synthesized via a one-pot two-step method: 6 mL of ssPEGss (0.0482 mmol) solution in DMF was dropped into 4 mL of HDI (0.0964 mmol) solution in DMF with stirring and then the mixture was stirred at 85 °C for 3 h. After cooling to 50 °C, 6 mL of DMSO dispersion containing a certain amount of H-CQD was quickly added and stirred at 75 °C for 6 h. Finally, the CQD(ssPEGss)n nanosponges were collected as light-yellow powder by dialysis (MW = 8000) with water for 5 days and then lyophilization.

The composition and structure of the CQD(ssPEGss)n nanosponges with pH/reduction dual-trigged disintegration were controlled by adjusting the mass feeding ratio of H-CQD and ssPEGss from 1:1 to 1:5, and the products were named CQD(ssPEGss)n-1:1, CQD(ssPEGss)n-1:2, CQD(ssPEGss)n-1:3, CQD(ssPEGss)n-1:4 and CQD(ssPEGss)n-1:5 respectively.

The carboxyl groups of CQD in the CQD(ssPEGss)n nanosponges were functionalized with hydrazine for the drug conjugation via acid-labile hydrazone (Scheme 1). 30 mg of CQD(ssPEGss)n-1:3 was dispersed in 30 mL DMSO. After adding 5 mL of hydrazine hydrate, the mixture was stirred for 24 h at room temperature. The light yellow Hy-CQD(ssPEGss)n nanosponge carrier was obtained by dialysis (MW = 8000) with water for 5 days and then lyophilization. Then, 16 mg of Hy-CQD(ssPEGss)n nanosponge were dispersed in 10 mL of DMSO. After adding DOX (20 mg), trifluoroacetic acid (20 μL) and trimethylamine (200 μL), the mixture was stirred for 24 h in dark at room temperature. Finally, the dark red DOX-Hy-CQD(ssPEGss)n nanosponges were separated via dialysis (MW = 8000) against DMSO for 1 day and then with water for 5 days and finally lyophilization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and characterization of CQD(ssPEGss)n nanosponges
ce:section.ce:para: The CQD(ssPEGss)n nanosponges was fabricated via the crosslinking of the isocyanate-terminated polyurethane prepolymer with the H-CQD, as shown in Scheme 1. The N-doped CQD was synthesized via the microwave-assisted pyrolysis [12], with well-defined spherical morphology of about 2 nm (Fig. 1a) and Dh around 3.5 nm (Fig. 2a). After the surface modification, the particle size and hydrodynamic diameter increased to 3 nm (Fig. 1b) and 6–9 nm (Fig. 2b) respectively, remaining the well-dispersed spherical morphology.

The isocyanate-terminated polyurethane prepolymer was synthesized by the end-group functionalization of the ssPEGss (Scheme S2), which was synthesized with a PEG:HDI molar ratio of 1:2, following with excessive bis(2-hydroxyethyl) disulfide. The chemical composition of PEG:HDI:bis(2-hydroxyethyl) disulfide could be revealed as 1:2:2 with the 1H NMR analysis (Fig. S1), demonstrating the stoichiometric reaction as expected.

With the similar strategy, the isocyanate-terminated polyurethane prepolymer were synthesized by end-group functionalization of ssPEGss with HDI at a molar ratio of 1:2, and then crosslinked with the H-CQD to form the CQD(ssPEGss)n nanosponges (Scheme 1). The CQD(ssPEGss)n nanosponges were obtained with unimodal diameter distribution with the mass feeding ratio of 1:3 and 1:4 (Fig. 2c and Fig. S2c), while the bimodal diameter distribution was resulted with other mass feeding ratio (Fig. S2). Furthermore, the CQD(ssPEGss)n-1:3 nanosponges possessed the smaller mean hydrodynamic diameter (Dh) around 80 nm with a narrower diameter distribution than the CQD(ssPEGss)n-1:4 nanosponges. So, the CQD(ssPEGss)n-1:3 nanosponges were selected for the further application.

The near spherical CQD(ssPEGss)n-1:3 nanosponges were obtained around 40 nm (Fig. 1c). The nanosponge structure could be clearly observed from the high magnification image. With the hydrophilic PEG-based block as linkers between the CQD-based crosslinking sites, the CQD(ssPEGss)n-1:3 nanosponges could be well swollen in water. As a result, the Dh was much bigger than the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) results. Such excellent swelling effect is expected to favor the drug conjugation on to the functionalized CQDs, because of the smaller space hindrance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and characterization of DOX-Hy-CQD(ssPEGss)n prodrug nanosponges
ce:section.ce:para: After the modifying the CQD(ssPEGss)n-1:3 nanosponges with hydrazine for the DOX conjugation via the acid-labile hydrazone bond, the Hy-CQD(ssPEGss)n remained the similar morphology and particle size as the CQD(ssPEGss)n (Fig. 1d), while their Dh increased to the range of 104–152 nm (Fig. 2d). The much higher swelling ratio was caused by the electrostatic repulsion of the positively charged hydrazine groups after the modification. Finally, the uniform spherical DOX-Hy-CQD(ssPEGss)n nanosponges in 60–70 nm were obtained after DOX conjugation (Fig. 1e). The significantly increased Dh in 153–180 nm (Fig. 2e) indicated the successful DOX conjugation with high drug content.

To determine the DOX content, the DOX-Hy-CQD(ssPEGss)n nanosponges were degraded completely by being dispersed into 1.0 M HCl containing 10 mM GSH and ultrasonicated for 96 h [13]. After centrifugation at 12000 rpm for 30 min, the DOX concentration in the supernatant was measured at 480 nm with UV–vis spectrometer. The DOX content was determined as 22.5%, higher than the reported CQD-based crosslinked organic hybrid DDSs in the form of prodrugs [12,14–16], and even the DDSs via non-covalent drug-loading [17]. The high drug content was attributed to the longer flexible PEG linkers between the CQDs.

The DOX-Hy-CQD(ssPEGss)n nanosponges also possessed good stability in the simulated normal physiological medium (pH 7.4 phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) + 10 μM GSH), with a slightly diameter increase from 168 nm to 186 nm after 12 h (Fig. 2f). It might be caused by the cleavage of some bioreducible disulfide bonds with the low GSH level in such medium.

The pH/hypoxia dual-triggered DOX release was then analyzed in different media (Supporting Information). There was no burst release in all the four cases (Fig. 3a). This is a remarkable feature of controlled drug release from the prodrugs in comparison with those DDSs via noncovalent drug loading. Most importantly, the DOX releasing rate was efficiently controlled by the acidity and GSH concentration of the media, showing excellent pH/hypoxia dual-triggered DOX release. The premature drug leakage in the simulated normal physiological medium was only 16% within 80 h, while the conjugated DOX could be completely released in the simulated tumor intracellular microenvironment.

The drug release mechanism was then fitted with both the Higuchi model and the Korsmeyer-Peppas model as shown in Fig. S3, and the main fitted parameters are presented in Table 1 . Clearly, the regression coefficient with the former one was higher than those with the later one, demonstrating that the Fickian diffusion mechanism of Higuchi model can better describe the drug release performance from the DOX-Hy-CQD(ssPEGss)n nanosponges [18]. Furthermore, the k value rose with increasing the media acidity and GSH level, further revealing the pH/hypoxia dual-triggered DOX release from the DOX-Hy-CQD(ssPEGss)n nanosponges.

To better explain the drug release, the diameter of the prodrug nanosponges was traced during the drug releasing process in the simulated tumor intracellular microenvironment. Clearly, the diameter became trimodal distribution centered at 120, 530, 1860 nm respectively, after 6 h of drug release (Fig. S4a). The smallest one should be the DOX-Hy-CQD(ssPEGss)n nanosponges after partial drug release caused by cleaving the hydrazone conjugation, while the other two should be the highly swollen nanosponges with less drug and lower crosslinking degree, caused by the acid-triggered drug release and GSH-triggered de-crosslinking of the bioreducible disulfide bonds and the acid-labile imine bonds. After 12 h with cumulative release >50%, a bimodal diameter could be seen (Fig. S4b), due to the disappearance of the DOX-Hy-CQD(ssPEGss)n nanosponges and the further swelling of the two bigger species in Fig. 4a due to the decreased crosslinking degree due to the continuous cleavage of the disulfide bonds. An obvious disintegration could be seen after 24 h with a cumulative drug release of >70%, in which there was only the degraded pieces around 1100 nm (Fig. S4c). Finally, the diameter of the degraded pieces decreased to 600 nm with cumulative release >93% after 48 h (Fig. S4d), revealing the pH/hypoxia dual-triggered disintegration of the carrier nanosponges during the drug release. The former one could facilitate the diffusion of the H+ ions into the nanosponges, favoring the cleavage of the drug conjugation and DOX release, simultaneously, promote the diffusion of the protonated DOX molecules out the carriers.

As a kind of biocompatible fluorescent nanomaterials with plentiful surface functional groups, CQD were used as building block to fabricate the prodrug nanosponges. Meantime, the fluorescent property was expected for real-time in-site imaging during the chemotherapy. The as-prepared CQD showed strong absorption at 238 and 350 nm, attributed to the π-π* transition of the CC bonds and the n-π* transition of the CO bonds, respectively (Fig. 4) [12]. The modification on their surface amino groups led to an intensity decrease of the two absorptions in the H-CQD. And the adsorption intensity further decreased after crosslinking with the isocyanate-terminated polyurethane prepolymer from their surface hydroxyl groups in the nanosponges. Obviously, the modification of the surface carboxyl groups in CQD with hydrazine caused a red shift of the n-π* transition from 250 nm to 360 nm in the Hy-CQD(ssPEGss)n. After DOX conjugation, the two characteristic absorption peaks of the CQD disappeared, while the DOX characteristic absorption at 290, 253 and 232 nm appeared and that at 480 nm shifted to 517 nm, due to the dynamic quenching by the surrounding conjugated DOX molecules [19].

The as-synthesized CQD showed excitation wavelength independent fluorescence at 450 nm with maximum excitation wavelength of 360 nm (Fig. 5a). They could emit bright blue fluorescence under 365 nm ultraviolet lamp (Fig. 5a, inset). Due to the static quenching by the surface chemical modification of the amino and carboxyl groups on the CQD and the dynamic quenching by the surrounding conjugated anthracyclines, as well as the decreased mass ratio of the CQD in the samples, the fluorescence intensity decreased during the fabrication of the proposed DOX-Hy-CQD(ssPEGss)n nanosponges (Fig. 5b-e). However, the fluorescence was partially recovered during DOX release (Fig. 5f). After 48 h, the Hy-CQD(ssPEGss)n nanosponges were disintegrated into small pieces, in which the imine bonds by the modification on their surface amino groups were acid-triggered cleaved. Such fluorescent property was expected for real-time in-site imaging during the tumor chemotherapy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cytotoxicity and intracellular distribution
ce:section.ce:para: The anti-tumor efficacy of the DOX-Hy-CQD(ssPEGss)n nanosponges was in vitro assessed on the human hepatoma cell line (HepG2) after incubation of 48 h (Supporting Information). The Hy-CQD(ssPEGss)n carrier nanosponges did not show obvious cytotoxicity on the HepG2 cells with survival rate of 89% even after incubation with a high dose of 20 μg/mL (Fig. 3b), demonstrating the good cytocompatibility of the Hy-CQD(ssPEGss)n carrier nanosponges.

Similar as the free DOX, the DOX-Hy-CQD(ssPEGss)n nanosponges exhibited a dose dependent cytotoxicity. In the determined dose range, the cell viability after incubation with the DOX-Hy-CQD(ssPEGss)n nanosponges at the same DOX-equivalent dose was lower than those with free DOX, indicating an enhanced tumor growth inhibition effect. And the IC50 values of free DOX and the proposed prodrug nanosponges were calculated as 10.20 and 10.08 μg/mL, respectively. It might be explained with the minimized P-glycoprotein-mediated drug efflux owing to the cellular internalization of the proposed prodrug nanosponges via endocytosis, while the free DOX was internalized via passive diffusion [12]. However, only slight cytotoxicity was found for the human hepatic cell line (L02) in comparison with free DOX, demonstrating the tumor-selective cytotoxicity of the proposed prodrug nanosponges owing to the bioreducible disulfide bonds.

The cellular uptake of the DOX-Hy-CQD(ssPEGss)n nanosponges and the intracellular distribution of the disintegrated products (DOX and CQD-contained small species) were analyzed with the confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) technique (Supporting Information). Clearly, the red fluorescence regions of DOX and the green fluorescence regions of the CQD-contained small species covered the blue fluorescence regions of the cell nuclei dyed with DAPI (Fig. 6 ). It demonstrated that the DOX-Hy-CQD(ssPEGss)n nanosponges could be internalized by the HepG2 cells and then disintegrate in the intracellular acidic/hypoxia medium. Finally, the released DOX and the disintegrated CQD-contained small species were accumulated mainly in the nuclei, inhibiting the cell proliferation and enabling the real-time imaging, respectively.

In summary, facile strategy was established as stoichiometric reaction to prepare the fluorescent traceable prodrug nanosponges for the tumor-specific pH/hypoxia dual-triggered drug delivery, via a high-performance crosslinking of the isocyanate-terminated polyurethane prepolymer with fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQD) as crosslinking blocks. The proposed DOX-Hy-CQD(ssPEGss)n nanosponges were obtained by conjugating DOX with the acid-labile hydrazone linker onto the Hy-CQD(ssPEGss)n carrier, which could be acid/GSH dual-triggered disintegrated inside the tumor cells. The DOX-Hy-CQD(ssPEGss)n nanosponges could be internalized by the tumor cells efficiently, showing enhanced selective tumor growth inhibition effect. Meantime, the partially recovered fluorescence of the disintegrated CQD-contained small species was expected for the real-time in-site imaging during drug release. The precise design of DDSs via the stoichiometric reaction provided a new insight for the potential tumor theranostic application.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors have no conflict of interest.

ce:section.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Gansu Province, China (Grant No. 18JR3RA271).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Supplementary material Other experimental details, 1H NMR spectrum, DLS results and fitted drug release plots.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colcom.2021.100576.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tumor-specific fluorescent Cdots-based nanotheranostics by acid-labile conjugation of doxorubicin onto reduction-cleavable Cdots-based nanoclusters
Mater. Sci. Eng. C
